headline: Bill Gates Releases Hundreds of Millions of Bacteria-Infected Mosquitoes Across Colombia

article_items.title: Bill Gates Releases Hundreds of Millions of Bacteria-Infected Mosquitoes Across Colombia
article_items.excerpt: A growing number of experts are raising the alarm over billionaire Bill Gates's extensive involvement in genetically modified mosquito projects after it emerged that the Microsoft co-founder has released hundreds of millions of bacteria-infested mosquitoes into the wild in Colombia. The revelation is drawing renewed scrutiny to Gates's other mosquito-based biological interventions around the world, with scientists warning that the long-term consequences remain largely unknown. The concerns intensified after the government of Burkina Faso terminated the controversial Target Malaria project last year, citing biosafety risks, questionable effectiveness, and concerns over scientific.
article_items.summary: Rather than genetically modifying the mosquitoes themselves, these programs breed mosquitoes carrying the bacteria and release them into the wild with the goal of spreading the organism through local mosquito populations. According to reports, approximately 30 million Wolbachia-infected mosquitoes are being released every single week as part of a large-scale program supported by the Gates Foundation and the World Mosquito Programme. The Foundation has also funded research involving Oxitec, a biotechnology company known for releasing genetically modified mosquitoes in various regions around the world. the Microsoft co-founder has released hundreds of millions of bacteria-infested mosquitoes into the wild in Colombia. According to reports, approximately 30 million Wolbachia-infected mosquitoes are being released every single week as part of a large-scale program supported by the Gates Foundation and the World Mosquito Programme.
the Microsoft co-founder has released hundreds of millions of bacteria-infested mosquitoes into the wild in Colombia.
Target Malaria launched operations in Burkina Faso in 2012 as part of a broader international effort to deploy gene-editing technologies against malaria-carrying mosquitoes.
The initiative began years ago and has expanded significantly, with supporters claiming the bacteria can reduce transmission of diseases such as dengue fever.

With millions of modified or bacteria-carrying mosquitoes already being released into communities around the world, opponents warn that society may not fully understand the consequences until years after the experiments.
Target Audience: The vaccines are indicated for children from 5 months of age.
Critics have warned that once released into the environment, such modifications could be impossible to reverse.
Critics argue that the increasing ability to genetically manipulate organisms raises important questions about oversight, transparency, and potential unintended consequences.
These vaccines target the Plasmodium falciparum parasite and are administered as part of routine childhood immunization in 25 African countries.
Effectiveness: Both vaccines reduce malaria cases by more than 50% in the first year and by approximately 75% when given seasonally in high-transmission areas.

rag_rules: Make the business move concrete: who is involved, what changed, and why now.
